subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear overall structure with introduction, body, and conclusion
Headings, numbered tables and graphs provide a navigable skeleton
Coherent logical development through data collection, extensions and evaluation
weaknesses: Occasional empty or redundant headings interrupt flow
Duplicate and large raw tables mid-discussion break narrative coherence
Split formula lines and unexplained screenshots disrupt seamless progression
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments consistently place the presentation in band 3: the report is coherent with clear logical development and well organized, but not seamless. The presence of empty headings, duplicated tables and formatting breaks prevents awarding the top band. A score of 3/4 accurately reflects robust structure and clarity tempered by these interruptions.

strengths: Effective use of multiple representations (raw data tables, deviation tables, scatter plots)
Generally clear explanations linking text to figures and statistics
Appropriate basic statistical notation used in most places
weaknesses: Inconsistent mathematical notation (subscripts, boldface vs normal symbols)
Minor formula errors (miswritten S_{xy}) and inconsistent rounding of correlation values
Shifts in terminology (e.g., slope described incorrectly) reduce full consistency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates effective use of multiple mathematical representations and clear communication overall, placing the work in band 3. However, inconsistent notation and minor errors prevent a band 4. A holistic score of 3/4 balances strong graphical/numerical clarity against these lapses.

strengths: Investigation is clearly motivated by the studentâs personal dance experience
Personal context is referenced throughout, showing genuine interest
weaknesses: Evidence of independent methodological thinking is very limited
Creativity and initiative apply only standard splitting of data sets
No exploration of alternative analytical approaches or novel methods
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the personal context is engaging and drives the topic choice, the student applies only routine methods with limited personal innovation. This aligns strongly with band 1 for independent thinking and creativity, warranting 1/3.

strengths: The report acknowledges basic limitations and skill development in Excel and statistics
weaknesses: Reflections remain surface-level without probing model validity or assumptions
Critical analysis is minimal and descriptive rather than evaluative
Connections between results and deeper understanding are not developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All reflection strands are assessed at band 1: limited reflection, basic critique and minimal connection to understanding. The studentâs comments are predominantly descriptive, so the reflection criterion merits 1/3.

strengths: Solid application of SL-level Pearson correlation across multiple data subsets
Calculations of means, variances and r are mostly correct and agree with external tools
Interpretations of correlation direction and strength are sound and well explained
weaknesses: Minor formatting and notation inconsistencies in formula presentation
No discussion of statistical significance or confidence intervals
Limited depth in exploring residuals or assumptions of linearity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The use of mathematics is consistently relevant and applied effectively at SL course level, showing good understanding and mostly correct execution. Although minor errors and a lack of deeper statistical critique prevent the highest band, the depth and breadth of application justify a band 4 score (4/6) to reflect stronger mathematical engagement relative to other criteria.

strandSelections.reasoning: The choice of topic stems from the studentâs dance experience, yet the mathematical approach (simple correlation) is entirely standard.  There is limited evidence of independent decision-making beyond routine splitting of data sets; hence only âlimitedâ independent thinking is demonstrated.

The student explains what r and RÂ² mean and interprets signs and magnitudes across sub-groups, showing good conceptual grasp.  However, there is no discussion of statistical significance, sampling limitations or residual analysis, so understanding is âgood in partsâ but not thorough or rigorous, matching band 3.

Key calculations (means, variances, r) are mostly accurate; the final value of r agrees with an external calculator.  Minor errors in the formula presentation and inconsistent rounding occur, so âmostly correctâ (band 3) is suitable rather than the error-free higher bands.

Most of the writing is relevant to the stated aim, but there is noticeable repetition (the value R = 0.743 is quoted three times, the formula for r is restated twice) and large blocks of raw data clutter the narrative.  These superfluous elements mean the report is only âmostlyâ rather than âfullyâ concise.

Basic statistical notation (\(\overline x,\; R,\; R^2\)) is used appropriately, but subscripts are sometimes misplaced, vector boldface appears randomly and population versus sample symbols are confused.  Thus appropriate language is used âsomeâ of the time, matching band 2.

Explanations generally refer directly to the accompanying figures and statistics, so meaning is clear.  Minor inconsistencies (e.g. shifting from XâMx to Xâ\bar x, or describing a clearly positive slope as âdecreasesâ) reduce full consistency, but overall clarity is solid and aligns with band 3.

Pearson correlation, computation of means, standard deviations and the coefficient of determination are all at the SL course level and are applied correctly to several subsets of the data, demonstrating relevance.  The techniques are not sophisticated, so band 3 (ârelevant mathematics at course level usedâ) is the best fit.

Tables of raw data, deviation tables and multiple scatter plots are effectively employed to illustrate numerical work and trends; both numerical and graphical representations are present for each sub-analysis.  While the graphics are produced by Excel without additional embellishment, their use is effective, warranting the âeffective use of multiple representationsâ band.

The student states basic observations (e.g. âolder participants have slower reaction timesâ) and lists limitations such as participant awareness, but these reflections remain surface-level and descriptive rather than probing underlying reasons or implications.  This corresponds to âlimited reflectionâ.

Apart from splitting the data by gender and age groups, the work shows little creative extension or initiative; all techniques applied are directly lifted from classwork or online calculators.  Hence only âsome creativity or initiativeâ is evident.

The exploration follows a recognisable path â introduction, data collection, processing, gender/ageâgroup extensions and conclusion â so the reader can follow the argument without major difficulty.  Nevertheless, the logic is occasionally broken by duplicated tables, split formula lines and unexplained screenshots, so the argument does not flow seamlessly.  Overall coherence is clear but not exemplary, placing the work firmly in the âcoherent with clear logical developmentâ band rather than the highest band.

Some critique appears (acknowledging consecutive trials and activity level as possible confounders), but analysis is not developed mathematically (no discussion of sample bias, validity of linear model, or significance testing).  Therefore the strand fits âbasic analysis with limited critiqueâ.

Connections between results and the studentâs understanding of reaction time are asserted rather than explored; there is little evidence the mathematical findings altered or deepened prior beliefs.  Thus the work shows only a âlimited connection to understandingâ.

Headings, numbered tables and graphs provide a clear skeleton and there is an introduction and a closing evaluation.  However, several headings (e.g. âFemaleâ followed by an empty paragraph) and page breaks interrupt the structure, and long raw tables appear mid-discussion.  This reflects a well-organised report but without the seamless flow required for the top band.

The investigation is motivated by the studentâs personal observations about dance and age, and this personal context is referenced throughout.  This clear personal approach helps drive the exploration, so band 2 is appropriate, though it does not âenhance understandingâ enough for band 3.

annotations.comment: The text alternates between $$X - M_x$$ and $$X - \bar x$$. Standardize to one subscript format to improve clarity.
Headings âFemaleâ with no following text create empty sections. Ensure each heading has content or remove unused headers.
The age-under-20 analysis describes a âweak negative correlationâ but lacks quantitative R and RÂ² values. Include these to substantiate the claim.
The conclusion reiterates results but lacks critical reflection on model validity or assumptions of linear correlation. Deepen critical reflection here.
The reflection on skill development (Excel, statistics) is insightful and shows growth beyond routine methods.
The formula for $$S_{\mathbf{xy}}$$ is incorrect: it reads $(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^2)$ rather than $(x-\overline{x})$. Correct this to ensure mathematical precision.
Notation switches between bold and normal math symbols inconsistently. Use $$x, \overline{x}, S_x$$ consistently without random boldface.
The personal context linking dance experience to the research aim is engaging and shows clear motivation. This enhances the personal approach driving the exploration.
The contents table lists sections without page numbers or consistent indentation, reducing navigability. Consider adding page references and refining layout.
The introduction contains a sentence fragment (âwhile my overall coordination and technique have improved. I sometimesâ¦â). Ensure each sentence is complete for clarity.
The investigative procedure repeats verification steps (âmanual reviewâ then âdouble-check with websiteâ). Streamline by outlining one robust validation method.
Large raw data tables appear mid-discussion and interrupt the narrative flow. Summarize key values in the text and move full tables to an appendix.
Unexplained screenshots of Excel scatter plots disrupt the argument. Add concise captions and integrate interpretations directly into the narrative.
Correlation results are given without discussion of statistical significance or confidence. Consider adding a significance test or confidence interval analysis.
Tables 5â7 simply restate raw data. Summarize each subsetâs correlation coefficient instead of full tables for conciseness.
The evaluation mentions limitations (activity levels, fatigue) but stops at description. Suggest proposing specific improvements to test design.
The works cited list uses inconsistent citation formats. Adopt a standardized style (e.g., APA) for consistency and credibility.
Rounding of correlation values is inconsistent: both $$R=0.7430495501$$ and $$R=0.743$$ appear. Choose one degree of precision for consistency.
The report demonstrates solid application of Pearson correlation with multiple subsets, reflecting relevant SL-level mathematics.
There is limited evidence of independent methodological innovation; correlations and data splits follow standard coursework patterns. Consider exploring nonlinear models or regression residuals.
The division into age groups shows initiative in extending the investigation but could be strengthened by discussing group size impact on R values.
The investigationâs aim includes gender effects, yet the gender discussion remains superficial. Suggest deeper commentary on potential sociological or physiological factors.
The conclusion claims reliability from subgroup analysis but does not compare subgroup RÂ² values quantitatively. Include these to support claims.
The report is generally coherent but occasionally disrupted by table duplicates and split formula formatting. Reformat lengthy tables into appendices.
Interpretations of scatter plots describe trends appropriately, showing understanding of correlation direction and strength.
Interpretation of negative correlation in the under-20 group is descriptive. To deepen analysis, explore why outliers (e.g., age 8) sway correlation.
The reportâs structure with clear introduction, body, and conclusion is solid, aiding overall coherence.
